# Laura Trujillo-Liñán (Philosophical Anthropology and Theory of Communication)

<!-- vimeo: 1111476486 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

[▶ Watch on Vimeo](https://vimeo.com/1111476486)

<details>
<summary>자막: Laura Trujillo-Liñán (Philosophical Anthropology and Theory of Communication)</summary>

Hello, I'm Laura Ian, vice president of the Media Ecology Association and professor at University of Pan Americana in Mexico City. I teach courses on humanities, philosophy, media ecology, and media theory. One of my main challenges is helping my students engage with complex texts and abstract ideas. I have been teaching university students for almost 20 years, and over time I have seen how much they change. Each new class that arrives comes with different context, lives, stories, and above all different media and technologies that shape the way they learn, think, and act. Because of this teaching, my courses has become increasingly complex, especially since they tend to be highly theoretical and demand considerable depth, particularly those related to philosophy. The greatest difficulties my students face are with long, dense philosophical texts from Aristotle, ma Malu, and others. They find the language and concepts challenging, which affect both their comprehension and their engagement. That's why I decided to use AI to improve the teaching and learning process. I decided to create custom GPTs, but my idea was not only for a students to use this technology, it was for them to learn how to build it. So I developed a step by step guide. In one of of the first classes. I explained what we were going to do and then we build the GPT together. The rule was simple. They could only use our class materials. We fed the GPT with the assigned readings for the course and instructed to answer questions only Using that material. I implemented the this process in both my communication and humanities groups. The next step was to design activities aligned with the learning objectives and I wanted to achieve through the GPT. The first exercise was reading comprehension. I asked students to read a section of the Summa Theological and be ready to explain it in class. The instructions were, read the text online, ask the GPTA specific questions about that exact paragraph, submit a written report explaining that section, and finally presented orally to the group. At the end, I asked them a Questions that is usually very complex to answer to my surprise, they explain it clearly and precisely without looking at at their notes. This was a topic I personally found difficult to teach, and one they normally took a long time to understand and especially to explain. The second exercise was in class debate. I divided the students into two groups, assigned each group a position, gave them five minutes to prepare their arguments with the GPT, and finally started the debate with a moderator asking questions and guiding the discussion. The answers were excellent. What struck me most was that to give these answers, they had to craft the right prompt and fully understand the GP T's responses. As the debate went on, they gradually stopped relying on the GPT and started generating high level responses on their own. That's when I realized working with the GPT have helped them not only understand the topic better, but also raise the level of, of discussion and truly generate knowledge themselves about the impact of using custom GPTs. I can talk about tricky points. Number one, greater class participation. Students interacted with the GPT during class and came prepared with better questions. Number two, better text comprehension. Their answers reflected deeper analysis and more precise use of concepts. And number three, time saved. GBTs reduced the time I spent answering repeated basic questions, freeing me to focus on higher level discussions. In a student service, they say they have learned how to personalize A GPT learned philosophy, and 90% found the GPTs helpful for understanding diff difficult text. In short, custom GPT have transformed how my students interact with complex ideas. They are now more confident, more engaged, and more autonomous learners. If you are an educator, I encourage you to experiment with ai. AI tools. Start small, but think big.

</details>
